13: prosdechomenoi ten makarian elpida kai epiphaneian tes doxes tou megalou theou kai soteros hemon Iesou Christou (prosdechomenoi tēn makarian elpida kai epiphaneian tēs doxēs tou megalou theou kai sōtēros hēmōn Iēsou Christou): 'waiting for our blessed hope, the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ.' The Greek syntax — tou megalou theou kai soteros hemon Iesou Christou — with the single definite article governing both 'God' and 'Savior' (the Granville Sharp rule) most naturally refers to one person: 'our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ.' This is one of the most explicit divine predications of Jesus in the NT — calling him directly 'our great God.' Epiphaneia (appearing/manifestation) is the distinctive Pastoral Christological term for the Incarnation (1 Tim 3:16; 2 Tim 1:10) and the parousia (1 Tim 6:14; 2 Tim 4:1, 8) — a term the Hellenistic world used for the appearance of a deity or the arrival of a king.
1: Sy de lalei ha prepei tē hygiainousē didaskalia (but you speak what is fitting for sound teaching): the adversative sy de (but you) sharply contrasts Titus with the defective teachers of ch. 1. Prepei (is fitting/appropriate) — the language of proper decorum; sound doctrine generates specific behavioral expectations that are "fitting" for it. Hygiainousa didaskalia (sound/healthy teaching) — the signature medical metaphor of the Pastoral Epistles; doctrine as health-giving rather than disease-producing.
2: Presbyterous nēphalious einai, semnous, sōphronas, hygiainontas tē pistei, tē agapē, tē hypomonē (older men to be temperate, dignified, self-controlled, sound in faith, in love, in endurance): the household code for older men. Nēphalios (temperate, sober — originally not drunk with wine, then broadly self-restrained). Semnos (dignified, honorable, worthy of respect) — the gravitas appropriate to age. The three objects of hygiainō (be sound in) — faith, love, endurance — form a triad that echoes the three theological virtues (faith, hope, love) with endurance substituting for hope, emphasizing the active dimension of sustained trust.
3: Presbyteridas hōsautōs en katastēmati hieroprepeis (older women likewise in bearing fitting for what is sacred): katastēma (bearing, deportment, manner of life) — the outward expression of the inward character. Hieroprepes (fitting for what is sacred/holy) — from hieros (sacred, holy) + prepō (to be fitting); the behavior appropriate to persons consecrated to God. Mē diabolous (not slanderers) — diabolos as adjective = slanderous, gossiping (the devil's characteristic work is slander, so the slanderer acts as the devil's instrument). Kalodidaskālous (teachers of what is good) — a hapax; the older women are teachers within their sphere — of the younger women (v. 4).
4: Hina sōphronizōsin tas neas philandrous einai, philoteknous (that they may train/encourage the younger women to love their husbands and children): sōphronizō (to make self-controlled, to train, to encourage toward wisdom) — the causative of sōphrōn; the older women impart the virtue of sound-mindedness to the younger. Philandrous (husband-lovers) and philoteknous (children-lovers) — compound adjectives formed on philos (loving); these are not commands imposed from outside but orientations formed by communal example and mentorship.
5: Sōphronas, hagnas, oikourgous agathas, hypotassomenas tois idiois andrasin, hina mē ho logos tou theou blasphēmētai (self-controlled, pure, working at home, good, subject to their own husbands, so that the word of God may not be blasphemed): oikourgous (workers at home, domestic managers) — the term signals the sphere of primary responsibility, not a general prohibition on public activity. Hina mē ho logos tou theou blasphēmētai (so that the word of God may not be slandered/blasphemed) — the missional rationale appears identically in 1 Tim 6:1; household order is a gospel concern because the watching world judges the teaching by the practitioners' lives.
6: Tous neoterους hōsautōs parakalei sōphronein (likewise encourage the younger men to be self-controlled): the single instruction for younger men is sōphronein (to be self-controlled, sound-minded) — the same root virtue that appears throughout the household code. The brevity is itself an observation: young men need one thing above all others. Hōsautōs (likewise, in the same manner) — the instruction for young men parallels and continues the household code that began with older men (v. 2) and older women (v. 3).
7: Peri panta seauton parecho typon kalōn ergōn, en tē didaskalia aphthorian, semnotēta (in everything present yourself as a model of good works, in teaching uncorruptness, dignity): typos (model, pattern, example) — the impression made by a die or seal that others replicate; Titus himself is to be the living pattern for young men to follow (cf. 1 Tim 4:12: "be an example to the believers"). Aphthoria (uncorruptness, integrity) in teaching — the quality of teaching untainted by self-interest, hidden agenda, or compromise. This is the only NT occurrence of aphthoria.
8: Logon hygiē, akatakriton, hina ho ex enantias entrapē mēden echōn legein peri hēmōn phaulon (sound speech that cannot be condemned, so that the opponent may be ashamed, having nothing bad to say about us): hygios logos (sound/healthy speech) — the health-metaphor extended to the manner of speech. Akatakritos (cannot be condemned, without accusation against it) — speech so unimpeachable that even the hostile critic finds no legitimate target. Ho ex enantias (the one from the opposite side, the opponent/adversary) — shame (entrapē) is the social consequence of failed attack; the opponent is put to shame by finding nothing to criticize.
9: Doulous idiois despotais hypotassesthai en pasin (slaves to be subject to their own masters in everything): the slave instruction in Titus is briefer than in Eph 6:5-8 or Col 3:22-25. The triple description — euarestous einai, mē antilegontas, mē nosphizomenous (to be pleasing, not talking back, not pilfering) — addresses the three specific behaviors most likely to undermine the slave's trustworthiness. Antilegō (to talk back, contradict, oppose in speech) — the verbal resistance that signals disrespect. Nosphizomai (to set aside for oneself, pilfer, embezzle) — from nosphis (apart, separately); the same word used of Ananias and Sapphira keeping back part of the proceeds (Acts 5:2-3).
10: Alla pistin pasan endeiknumenous agathēn, hina tēn didaskalian tēn tou sōtēros hēmōn theou kosmōsin en pasin (but showing all good faith/trustworthiness, so that they may adorn the teaching of our God and Savior in everything): pistin agathēn (good faithfulness/trustworthiness) — pistis here as reliability rather than saving faith; the slave who is completely trustworthy. Kosmeō (to adorn, decorate, arrange beautifully) — the slave's trustworthy conduct is a cosmetic act: it makes the teaching beautiful to the watching world. From this verb comes "cosmetic" and "cosmos" (the ordered universe). The teaching of the gospel is adorned or disfigured by the behavior of those who profess it.
11: Epephanē gar hē charis tou theou sōtērios pasin anthrōpois (for the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all people): the great christological declaration that grounds the household code (vv. 2-10) in salvation-history. Epiphanē (appeared, was manifested) — the aorist of epiphainō; the appearing of the Incarnation. Charis tou theou sōtērios (the saving grace of God / the grace of God that brings salvation) — grace personified as appearing. Pasin anthrōpois (to all people) — the universal scope of salvific grace; not limited by ethnicity, class, or gender (the household code has addressed all: men, women, slaves).
12: Paideuousa hēmas hina arnēsamenoi tēn asebeian kai tas kosmikais epithumias sōphronōs kai dikaiōs kai eusebōs zēsōmen en tō nyn aiōni (instructing us that, having denied ungodliness and worldly desires, we may live self-controlled, righteously, and godly in the present age): paideuousa (instructing, training, disciplining) — grace is personified as a teacher-disciplinarian; it does not merely forgive but forms. The three adverbs — sōphronōs (self-controlled, sound-mindedly: inward orientation), dikaiōs (righteously: horizontal/social orientation), eusebōs (godly: vertical/divine orientation) — cover the three axes of the virtuous life. The renunciation of asebeia (ungodliness) and kosmikai epithumiai (worldly desires) is the negative counterpart to the triple-virtue life.
14: Hos edōken heauton hyper hēmōn hina lytrōsētai hēmas apo pasēs anomias kai katharisē heautō laon periousion (who gave himself for us to redeem us from all lawlessness and to purify for himself a people of his own possession): the two purpose-clauses of the self-giving. Lytrōsētai (to ransom/redeem) — the ransom-payment vocabulary (from lytron = the price of redemption); freedom from anomia (lawlessness) is the result. Laon periousion (a people of his own possession/a treasured people) — periousion is the LXX translation of Hebrew segullah (treasured possession, special property) in Exod 19:5; Deut 7:6; 14:2; 26:18: Israel as YHWH's special possession is now applied to the church purified by Christ. Zēlōtēn kalōn ergōn (zealous for good works) — the purified people are defined by their eagerness for good works, not by ethnic or ritual status.
15: Tauta lalei kai parakalei kai elegche meta pasēs epitagēs (speak these things and encourage and rebuke with all authority): the three imperatives (lalei, parakalei, elegche) cover the full ministerial range: proclamation, encouragement, and correction. Epitagē (authority, command, directive) — the ministerial authority that grounds Titus's teaching is apostolic in origin (derived from Paul and ultimately from God). Mēdeis sou periphronitō (let no one disregard/despise you) — periphronō = to think around or beyond something, to dismiss it as beneath notice; the same verb as 1 Tim 4:12 for Timothy's youth. Titus's authority is not self-asserted but grounded in the apostolic word he carries.
